title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Hidden Cost of Constant Connectivity
content.subtitle: How the always-on culture is quietly eroding our capacity for the kind of thinking that matters most.
content.text: There is a particular kind of work that cannot be done in five-minute pockets between notifications: the sustained, demanding, single-minded concentration required to produce anything of lasting value. A mathematician cannot solve a complex proof in fragments; a novelist cannot build a coherent world in between checking emails. Yet modern professional life has been redesigned—almost entirely without deliberate intention—to make this kind of focused work extraordinarily difficult. [31] The writer argues that the greatest cognitive threat of the digital age is not the quality of the information we consume, but the systematic destruction of our ability to focus on anything for long enough to truly understand it. [/31] We are not drowning in information; we are drowning in interruption. || The mechanisms driving this are not accidental. The platforms and devices we use every day are engineered to capture and hold attention. Each notification, each new message, each algorithmically curated feed exploits the same neurological pathway as a slot machine—the unpredictable reward schedule that keeps a gambler at the lever. [32] The critical difference between the casino and the smartphone is that the smartphone is in our pockets at all times, and its presence alone—even when turned face-down and silent—has been shown to measurably reduce the available cognitive bandwidth of those nearby. [/32] We are paying an attention tax simply by coexisting with our devices. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the phenomenon of 'shallow work' in professional organisations is one of resigned frustration. [/33] Many organisations have inadvertently structured their operations around activities that are easy to perform while distracted: replying to emails, attending meetings, generating reports that are read by no one. These activities create a satisfying illusion of busyness and productivity while displacing the deeper work that would actually move an organisation forward. The inbox becomes a substitute for ambition. A full calendar becomes a mask for intellectual avoidance. || Reclaiming the capacity for focused work is not merely a matter of turning off one's phone. [34] Research on cognitive recovery suggests that after a period of intensive multitasking and digital distraction, the prefrontal cortex can require several hours of genuine quiet to return to its full capacity for abstract reasoning and creative problem-solving. [/34] This has significant implications for how we structure our working days. Brief 'digital detoxes' of twenty minutes are not sufficient; meaningful restoration requires long, unbroken blocks of time protected from all forms of digital intrusion. || [35] Some commentators suggest that the capacity for deep focus is becoming a rare and highly valuable professional skill—one that will increasingly define the boundary between those who produce exceptional work and those who merely process information. [/35] If this is correct, the individuals and organisations that learn to protect and cultivate focused attention will hold a decisive competitive advantage over those who remain mired in distraction. In an economy that increasingly rewards the creation of new ideas over the execution of routine tasks, the ability to think deeply and at length may become the most important professional capability of the century. || Ultimately, the path back to focused work requires a cultural shift as much as a personal one. [36] The writer concludes that individuals cannot be expected to resist, alone and through sheer willpower, systems that have been designed by teams of engineers to be maximally addictive. [/36] Structural changes—to workplace norms, to digital platform design, and to the educational frameworks that shape young minds—are required alongside individual effort. The goal is not to reject technology, but to recover our sovereignty over our own attention and, by extension, the quality of the work and thought of which we are genuinely capable.

questions.question: What does the author identify as the primary cognitive danger of modern digital life in the first paragraph?
options: A) The poor quality and inaccuracy of the information available online.
B) The loss of our ability to concentrate deeply for a sustained period.
C) The excessive amount of time people spend on social media platforms.
D) The decline in people's willingness to read books and long articles.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the description of the 'systematic destruction of our ability to focus.' A is explicitly rejected ('We are not drowning in information'). C and D are not the primary danger identified.

questions.question: How does the author distinguish between the casino and the smartphone in the second paragraph?
options: A) The casino is more addictive because the financial rewards are larger.
B) The smartphone uses a different neurological mechanism than the casino.
C) The smartphone is more intrusive because it is always present and affects us passively.
D) The casino causes more harm to cognitive performance than the smartphone.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the point that the smartphone 'is in our pockets at all times' and that its mere presence 'measurably reduces the available cognitive bandwidth.' A and D are not mentioned. B is the opposite; both exploit the same neurological pathway.

questions.question: What is the author's view of activities like answering emails and attending meetings?
options: A) They are essential for maintaining team cohesion and communication.
B) They provide a false sense of productivity while replacing more valuable work.
C) They represent the most efficient use of time in a modern organisation.
D) They are more mentally demanding than the author gives them credit for.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33] and the description of these activities as creating 'a satisfying illusion of busyness' while 'displacing the deeper work.' A, C, and D contradict the author's tone of 'resigned frustration.'

questions.question: What does the research on 'cognitive recovery' described in the fourth paragraph imply for the working day?
options: A) A twenty-minute break from screens is enough to fully restore concentration.
B) People should work for only four hours a day to avoid cognitive fatigue.
C) Long, uninterrupted periods of quiet are needed to restore full mental capacity.
D) The brain recovers most effectively when people sleep between work sessions.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the explicit statement that 'meaningful restoration requires long, unbroken blocks of time.' A is explicitly rejected ('Brief digital detoxes of twenty minutes are not sufficient'). B and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: According to the fifth paragraph, what competitive advantage might deep focus provide in future?
options: A) It will allow workers to complete routine tasks more quickly than their rivals.
B) It will become irrelevant as artificial intelligence takes over creative work.
C) It will distinguish those who produce exceptional work from those who only process information.
D) It will be recognised as a rare skill but will not affect professional outcomes.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35] and the claim that deep focus 'will increasingly define the boundary between those who produce exceptional work and those who merely process information.' A, B, and D are not suggested or are directly contradicted.

questions.question: What does the writer ultimately argue is needed to restore our capacity for focused work?
options: A) Individual willpower and personal responsibility are entirely sufficient.
B) Technology companies should be banned from designing addictive products.
C) People should completely reject all digital technology in their personal lives.
D) Both personal effort and structural changes to systems and norms are required.
questions.explanation: D is supported by [36] and the conclusion that 'structural changes—to workplace norms, to digital platform design, and to educational frameworks—are required alongside individual effort.' A is explicitly rejected. B and C are more extreme than what the author recommends.
